original_abstract: Despite the vast potential of porous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), their integration into electronic devices has remained limited, primarily due to the scarcity of electrically conductive variants. In this work, we introduce conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs as a versatile class of materials for the chemiresistive detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). We demonstrate that a series of structurally analogous 2D MOFs can be effectively organized into a cross-reactive sensor array, enabling the clear discrimination of diverse VOC categories. Our experimental results reveal the presence of multiple, highly orthogonal sensing mechanisms, suggesting that these 2D MOFs are mechanistically distinct from traditional chemiresistive materials. These findings highlight the unique advantages of conductive MOFs and establish a robust foundation for their use in next-generation chemical sensing technologies.
humanized_abstract: While porous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) possess tremendous potential, their integration into electronic devices is generally very restricted, especially towards electrically conductive varieties. In this work, we demonstrate for the first time the applications of the new class of materials conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs as building blocks towards affordable, flexible and chemiresistive volatile organic compounds (VOCs) sensors. By controlling the structures of a series of chemically similar 2D MOFs with different electrical and catalytic characteristics while preserving their inherent structural functionality, and arranging them in a cross-reactive sensor array, excellent selectivities for different VOCs (volatile organic compounds) were achieved. Upon investigation, our experimental data confirm multiple orthogonal sensing mechanisms for every individual conductive 2D MOF, which signifies that 2D MOFs possess distinct characteristics compared with other conventional chemiresistive materials. This is in good harmony with the inherent advantages and unique functionality of the new class of material – the electrically conductive MOFs.
